skill.name: designing-apis
skill.description: Design REST and GraphQL APIs with resource models, endpoint structure, response formats, errors, authentication, rate limits, and OpenAPI documentation.
skill.summary: Guides API design from resource modeling through OpenAPI documentation.
skill.author: CloudAI-X

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are markdown code fences, REST method examples, OpenAPI references, and authentication documentation. No prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or executable malware behavior was found. The remaining concern is optional guidance to run external npx validation tools, which creates a moderate supply-chain risk if users execute it without pinning or review.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Validation Commands
medium_findings.description: The OpenAPI template recommends running npx-based validation and linting commands. This is legitimate API documentation, but executing external CLI packages can run third-party code and should be reviewed or pinned before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in a documentation section and are not hidden or automatically executed. Risk remains because npx can fetch and execute external packages when a user follows the instructions.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Misclassified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: The Ruby backtick findings point to markdown fenced examples, not Ruby code or automatic shell execution. The content is instructional text for API design and OpenAPI templates.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears inside markdown fences and surrounding text describes examples. There is no Ruby source file, interpreter call, or automatic execution path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts map to API terminology, JWT bearer examples, and API key documentation. No code performs cryptography, stores secrets, or selects an insecure algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are explanatory authentication examples in markdown. I found no cryptographic implementation or operational use of a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: Example API URL Is Not Network Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL is an example server value in an OpenAPI template. The skill does not make network requests or send data to that URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL uses the reserved example.com domain in a YAML documentation example. No executable network client or data transfer logic is present.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Detections Are HTTP Examples
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance findings align with REST endpoint examples and response metadata, not commands that inspect a host or environment. No evidence found of system inventory or local information gathering.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed content consists of REST routes, HTTP methods, and API design checklist items. I found no system commands, host probes, or filesystem enumeration.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Package Execution Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The template includes commands that may execute third-party packages for OpenAPI validation. Users should prefer pinned versions or trusted local tooling.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command pattern is real, but it appears as transparent documentation rather than hidden execution. The risk depends on whether a user runs the commands and how packages are resolved.

content.user_title: Design Clear REST and GraphQL APIs
content.value_statement: API projects often fail when contracts, errors, and versioning are decided too late. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured workflow for practical API design.
actual_capabilities: Creates REST endpoint structures using resource-oriented URL patterns.
Defines standard success, list, and error response formats.
Recommends HTTP status codes for common API outcomes.
Outlines authentication, authorization, and rate limit documentation.
Provides GraphQL query, mutation, payload, and input patterns.
Supplies an OpenAPI 3.0 template for documenting a user API.
limitations: Does not generate production server code or database migrations.
Does not validate OpenAPI files unless the user runs separate tooling.
Uses generic examples that must be adapted to product requirements.
Does not replace security review for authentication and authorization logic.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Product API
use_cases.description: Define resources, endpoints, responses, errors, authentication, and documentation before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Review an Existing API Contract
use_cases.description: Check endpoint naming, pagination, versioning, status codes, and error formats for consistency.

use_cases.title: Document API Behavior
use_cases.description: Create a practical OpenAPI starting point that captures paths, schemas, security schemes, and common responses.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic REST API
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a simple resource API.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a REST API for [product area]. Include resources, endpoints, request fields, response formats, and common errors.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Endpoint Consistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these API endpoints for REST consistency, pagination, status codes, and error handling: [paste endpoints].

prompt_templates.title: Design REST and GraphQL Contracts
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced design for teams supporting both styles.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design both REST endpoints and a GraphQL schema for [domain]. Include mutations, payloads, filters, pagination, and authorization notes.

prompt_templates.title: Produce an OpenAPI Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an OpenAPI documentation plan for [API]. Include paths, schemas, security schemes, responses, versioning, and validation steps.

output_examples.output: A resource model for users, role assignments, and sessions, plus endpoint names, response conventions, error cases, and authentication notes.

output: Renames action-based routes to resource-based routes.
Adds pagination expectations for list endpoints.
Standardizes validation and not-found error responses.

output_examples.input: Plan API documentation for a billing service.
output_examples.output: A documentation outline covering invoice and payment resources, security schemes, response models, rate limits, and validation tooling.

best_practices: Start with resources and relationships before choosing endpoint paths.
Define error formats and status code rules before implementation.
Keep OpenAPI documentation synchronized with product behavior.
anti_patterns: Mixing verbs and nouns in endpoint names without a clear rule.
Returning different response shapes for similar resources.
Adding authentication details after public contracts are already published.

faq.question: Does this skill support both REST and GraphQL?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes REST endpoint patterns and GraphQL schema patterns for queries, mutations, inputs, and payloads.

faq.question: Can it generate an OpenAPI specification?
faq.answer: It provides an OpenAPI 3.0 template and guidance. The final specification still needs project-specific details.

faq.question: Does it run API validation tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It only documents optional validation commands that a user may choose to run.

faq.question: Is it useful for existing APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review existing routes, response formats, pagination, error handling, and versioning choices.

faq.question: Does it handle authentication design?
faq.answer: It covers common documentation patterns for bearer tokens, API keys, authorization checks, and protected endpoints.

faq.question: Can it replace a security review?
faq.answer: No. It provides API design guidance, but teams must still review access control and data exposure risks.
